text: The catalytic performance of supported metal nanoparticles is fundamentally governed by the dynamic evolution of their active sites under reaction conditions. In this work, we report a light-induced strategy to manipulate the structural and compositional restructuring of Cu species on a TiO₂ support during low-temperature methanol steam reforming (MSR). We observe that under dark conditions, the catalyst undergoes rapid thermal deactivation as the active metallic Cu sites are oxidatively restructured into inoperative Cu₂O by strongly bound *CH₃O intermediates. However, the introduction of UV light triggers a restorative transformation, where photo-excited charge carriers from the TiO₂ support reduce the Cu₂O back to the active metallic state. This in situ structural regulation results in a dramatic enhancement of both catalytic activity and long-term stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO₂ catalyst achieves an H₂ production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, surpassing most reported noble-metal-based and conventional thermocatalytic systems. Combined operando characterizations and DFT-based molecular dynamics simulations provide atomistic insights into the mechanism of this charge-carrier-mediated restructuring. These findings highlight the potential of utilizing light–matter–reactant interactions to achieve real-time functionalization and stabilization of non-noble-metal catalysts for sustainable energy applications.
